_provenance: Claude-authored original Gaokao reading, batch D4. ~220-280 word passages, 4 MCQs each (detail/inference/main idea/attitude/vocab). Gaokao difficulty. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: James grew up in a poor part of the city, where good food and bright futures both seemed in short supply. As a teenager, he often got into trouble, and few people expected much of him. Then, almost by chance, he found a part-time job washing dishes in a small restaurant. There, watching the cooks work, he discovered something that would change his life: a love of cooking. He had found his passion, but the road was hard. He could not afford cooking school, so he learned by watching, asking questions, and practising late into the night. He made countless mistakes, and more than once he was told he would never make it. But James refused to give up. Cooking, he found, gave him a sense of pride and purpose he had never known. Years later, James opened his own small restaurant. But he did something unusual with it. Remembering his own difficult youth, he made it his mission to employ and train young people from troubled backgrounds, just as someone had once given him a chance. Many of them, like him, had been written off by others. Today, James's restaurant is admired not only for its food but for the lives it has changed. Dozens of young people who once had no hope have learned a skill, found work, and rebuilt their futures. James often says that the best dish he ever made was not on any menu: it was a second chance, served to those who needed it most.

questions.prompt: How did James first discover his love of cooking?
options: While washing dishes in a small restaurant.

questions.prompt: How did James learn to cook?
options: By watching, asking and practising, since he could not afford school.

questions.prompt: What unusual thing did James do with his restaurant?
options: He employed and trained young people from troubled backgrounds.

questions.prompt: What does James call the 'best dish' he ever made?
options: A second chance, given to those who needed it most.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 在小餐馆洗碗时)、细节(B 上不起学、靠观察练习自学)、细节(B 雇用并培训问题青年)、主旨(B 最好的'菜'是给最需要者的第二次机会)。

sets.passage: Plastic pollution is usually pictured as bottles and bags floating in the sea. But scientists are increasingly worried about a form of plastic pollution we cannot even see: microplastics. These are tiny pieces of plastic, smaller than a grain of rice, and they are now found almost everywhere on Earth. Microplastics come from many sources. Larger plastic items break down into smaller and smaller pieces under the sun and waves. Synthetic clothes release tiny fibres every time they are washed. Some products even contain plastic beads on purpose. Once released, these particles are carried by wind and water to the most remote places, from the deepest oceans to mountain snow. The most worrying question is what they do to living things. Microplastics have been found inside fish, in the bodies of animals, and even in human blood. Yet here lies the problem: because the issue is so new, scientists cannot yet say with confidence how much harm, if any, these particles cause. This uncertainty is itself a reason for concern. Cleaning up microplastics is nearly impossible; once they are spread through the environment, they cannot be gathered up again. This means prevention is the only real answer. Reducing our use of plastic, designing products more carefully, and improving how we treat waste water all help to stop microplastics at the source. The tiny size of the problem, it turns out, makes it all the harder to solve.

questions.prompt: What are microplastics?
options: Tiny pieces of plastic, smaller than a grain of rice.

questions.prompt: Where do microplastics come from?
options: Broken-down plastic, washed synthetic clothes, and added beads.

questions.prompt: Why can't scientists yet say how harmful they are?
options: Because the issue is so new that the harm is still uncertain.

questions.prompt: Why is prevention the only real answer?
options: Because they are easy to gather up.
Because once spread, they cannot be gathered up again.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 比米粒还小的塑料碎片)、细节(B 碎裂塑料/洗涤合成衣/添加微珠)、细节(B 问题太新、危害尚不确定)、主旨/推断(B 扩散后无法收集、故只能源头预防)。

sets.passage: A generation ago, most people worked in one place: an office owned by their employer, where they sat at the same desk for years. Today, a very different model is spreading: the shared workspace, where people from many different companies, or working for themselves, rent a desk in a common building. The reasons for this change are several. The rise of remote work and freelancing means that more people no longer have a fixed office. Working alone at home, however, can be lonely and full of distractions. Shared workspaces offer a solution: a professional place to work, with the equipment and quiet that home may lack, but also the company of others. Supporters say the benefits go beyond convenience. In a shared space, a web designer might sit beside a writer, an engineer beside an artist. This mixing of people from different fields, they argue, sparks new ideas and useful connections that would never happen in a traditional office full of people doing the same job. Of course, the model does not suit everyone. Some find the open, busy environment distracting, and the lack of privacy can be a problem. Yet the growth of shared workspaces reflects a deeper change in how we think about work itself: less as a fixed place we go to, and more as something we can do anywhere, in the company we choose. The office, it seems, is being quietly reinvented.

questions.prompt: What is a shared workspace?
options: A common building where people from many companies rent desks.

questions.prompt: Why has this model spread?
options: Remote work and freelancing rose, and home can be lonely and distracting.
Companies forced everyone to use it.

questions.prompt: What benefit do supporters claim?
options: It is always quiet and private.
Mixing people from different fields sparks new ideas and connections.
Everyone does exactly the same job.

questions.prompt: What deeper change does it reflect?
options: That all offices will close down.
That work is becoming something we can do anywhere, not a fixed place.
That working at home is always best.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 多公司共用、租工位的楼)、细节(B 远程与自由职业兴起、在家孤独易分心)、细节(B 跨领域混合激发新点子)、主旨/推断(B 工作正变成可随处进行而非固定地点)。

sets.passage: We do not usually think of plants as clever. They cannot move or think as animals do, and they seem simply to sit and grow. Yet scientists are discovering that plants are far more capable than we ever imagined, able to sense their surroundings and respond in surprisingly active ways. Plants, it turns out, can 'feel' the world around them. They sense light, gravity, and touch, and they grow towards what they need. Some can tell the difference between the shadow of a passing cloud and the shade of a competing plant, and respond accordingly. When attacked by insects, certain plants release chemicals into the air — a kind of warning that nearby plants of the same kind can detect, preparing their own defences before the insects arrive. Even more remarkably, plants appear to communicate and cooperate underground. Through vast networks of fungi connecting their roots, trees in a forest can share water and nutrients, and older 'mother trees' may even support their own young. A forest, in this view, is not a collection of separate individuals competing for survival, but something closer to a single, connected community. None of this means that plants think or feel as we do. But it does challenge our old idea of plants as passive, simple things. The more closely we look, the more the natural world reveals an intelligence — of a different kind from our own — woven through every living thing.

questions.prompt: What can plants sense, according to the passage?
What do some plants do when attacked by insects?
options: They release chemicals that warn nearby plants.

questions.prompt: How do trees cooperate underground?
options: They do not cooperate at all.
They share water and nutrients through networks of fungi.

questions.prompt: What old idea does this research challenge?
options: The old idea of plants as passive, simple things.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 感知光/重力/触碰)、细节(B 释放化学物质警告同类)、细节(B 借真菌网络分享水与养分)、主旨/推断(B 挑战'植物被动而简单'的旧观念)。

sets.passage: Before the internet, if you wanted to choose a restaurant or buy a product, you relied on advertisements, the advice of friends, or simple luck. Today, we have a powerful new tool: online reviews. With a few taps, we can read the opinions of thousands of strangers who have tried something before us. This has changed shopping, travel and dining in profound ways. The benefits are clear. Reviews give ordinary people a voice and hold businesses to account. A restaurant that treats customers badly can no longer hide it; a useless product will quickly gather warnings. For shoppers, reviews offer valuable information that was once impossible to find. Yet the system has serious weaknesses. Reviews can be faked: some businesses pay for glowing praise, or post bad reviews about their rivals. People are also more likely to write a review when they are angry than when they are merely satisfied, which can give a misleadingly negative picture. And a single unfair review can do real harm to a small business. The wise approach, then, is to treat online reviews as helpful but imperfect. Reading many reviews rather than one, ignoring both the most extreme praise and the most extreme complaints, and using our own judgement all help. Reviews are a useful guide, but they are not the truth itself. In the end, the most important opinion remains our own.

questions.prompt: What did people rely on before online reviews?
options: Advertisements, the advice of friends, and luck.

questions.prompt: What is one benefit of online reviews?
options: They give ordinary people a voice and hold businesses to account.
They are written only by experts.

questions.prompt: What is one weakness of online reviews?
options: They can be faked or unfairly negative.

questions.prompt: What is the wise approach to reviews?
options: Treat reviews as helpful but imperfect, and use our own judgement.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 广告/朋友建议/运气)、细节(B 给普通人发声、监督商家)、细节(B 评论可造假或失实负面)、主旨/推断(B 视评论为有用但不完美、并用自己的判断)。

sets.passage: When Chen Lan inherited her family's farm, everyone expected her to run it as her father had, using the chemicals and methods of modern industrial farming. Instead, she made a decision that shocked her neighbours: she would farm without chemicals, working with nature rather than against it. At first, her choice seemed foolish. Her yields fell, insects attacked her crops, and for several hard years she struggled to make a living. Other farmers shook their heads, certain she would fail. But Chen Lan was patient. She planted a variety of crops instead of just one, encouraged the insects and birds that ate the pests, and slowly rebuilt the health of her soil. Gradually, things changed. As the soil grew richer and the natural balance returned, her farm became stronger and more productive. Her vegetables, grown without chemicals, were healthier and tastier, and customers were willing to pay more for them. Within ten years, her once-struggling farm was thriving. Chen Lan's success has not gone unnoticed. Some of the very neighbours who once doubted her have begun to copy her methods. She now spends part of her time teaching other farmers what she has learned. Her story is not only about one farm, but about a larger truth: that working patiently with nature, rather than trying to force it, can in the end be both wiser and more rewarding than the quick, easy path.

questions.prompt: What shocking decision did Chen Lan make?
options: To farm without chemicals, working with nature.
To grow only a single crop.

questions.prompt: What happened on her farm at first?
options: Her yields fell and she struggled for several years.

questions.prompt: Why were customers willing to pay more?
options: Her vegetables were healthier and tastier.

questions.prompt: What larger truth does her story show?
options: That working patiently with nature can be wiser and more rewarding.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 不用化学品、顺应自然耕作)、细节(B 起初减产、苦撑数年)、细节(B 无化学品蔬菜更健康美味)、主旨(B 耐心顺应自然比走捷径更明智也更有回报)。

sets.passage: When electronic books, or e-books, first appeared, many predicted the end of the printed book. Why carry heavy paper, they asked, when a single small device could hold thousands of titles? For a while, e-book sales rose quickly, and the death of print seemed near. Yet something unexpected has happened: the printed book has not died, and in some places its sales are even growing again. E-books have undeniable advantages. They are light and portable, can be bought instantly, and let readers carry an entire library in their bag. For travellers and students, this is a real benefit. The text can be made larger for those with poor eyesight, and some readers simply prefer reading on a screen. But printed books offer something digital ones cannot. Many readers say they remember and concentrate better with a physical book, free from the distractions of a device that can also send messages and show videos. A printed book can be lent, given as a gift, or displayed on a shelf. There is also, for many, a simple physical pleasure in holding a book and turning its pages. The likely future is not a battle in which one wins and the other dies, but a peaceful coexistence. Different formats suit different readers, different moments, and different kinds of reading. The printed book, far from being made obsolete, has found its place beside the screen, proving that old technologies do not always disappear when new ones arrive.

questions.prompt: What did many people predict when e-books appeared?
options: The end of the printed book.

questions.prompt: What is an advantage of e-books?
options: They are light, portable and instant, and the text can be enlarged.

questions.prompt: What do printed books offer that e-books cannot?
options: Better concentration and a physical pleasure; they can be lent or given as gifts.

questions.prompt: What is the likely future of the two formats?
options: A peaceful coexistence of printed and electronic books.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 预言纸书将终结)、细节(B 轻便、即时、可放大字体)、细节(B 更专注、可借赠、翻页之乐)、主旨/推断(B 两者将和平共存)。

sets.passage: For decades, cities were built mainly for cars. Roads grew wider, car parks multiplied, and cyclists were left to risk their lives among the traffic. Recently, however, a growing number of cities have begun to change direction, building safe, separate lanes for bicycles. The results have been striking. The benefits of cycling are well known. It produces no pollution, eases traffic, takes up little space, and keeps people healthy. Yet for years, few people cycled in many cities, not because they did not want to, but because they were afraid. Sharing busy roads with fast cars and large trucks simply felt too dangerous. The key discovery has been simple but powerful: build safe lanes, and people will cycle. When cities create protected bike lanes, separated from car traffic, the number of cyclists rises dramatically, often including people who had never dared to cycle before — children, the elderly, and ordinary commuters. A street once choked with cars can become a pleasant, moving river of bicycles. Of course, change is not always easy. Some drivers resent giving up road space, and businesses sometimes fear losing customers. Yet city after city has found that, once the lanes are built and the cyclists appear, the complaints fade and the benefits become clear. The lesson is encouraging: by changing how we design our streets, we can change how people choose to travel, and make our cities cleaner, safer and more human in the process.

questions.prompt: How were cities built for decades?
Why did few people cycle in many cities?
options: They were afraid of sharing busy roads with cars and trucks.

questions.prompt: What is the 'key discovery' mentioned?
options: That if cities build safe lanes, the number of cyclists rises dramatically.

questions.prompt: What is the encouraging lesson?
options: That cars can never be replaced.
That changing how we design streets can change how people travel.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 主要为汽车而建)、细节(B 怕与汽车货车共道太危险)、细节(B 修了安全车道、骑行者剧增)、主旨(B 改变街道设计能改变出行方式)。

sets.passage: Scattered around the world are a few small regions where people live remarkably long and healthy lives, with an unusual number reaching a hundred years of age. Scientists have studied these places closely, hoping to discover the secret of long life. What they have found is both surprising and, in a way, reassuring. There is no single magic food or medicine behind this longevity. Instead, the long-lived share a set of simple, everyday habits. They eat moderately, with diets rich in vegetables and beans and low in meat and sugar. They stay physically active, not through hard exercise at a gym, but through the natural movement of daily life: walking, gardening, and working with their hands well into old age. Just as important, scientists believe, are factors that have nothing to do with diet or exercise. In these communities, old people are respected and remain part of family and social life, rather than being left alone. Strong friendships and a sense of belonging are woven into daily life. People also tend to have a clear sense of purpose, a reason to get up in the morning. The lesson of these long-lived communities is strangely ordinary. The secret of a long, healthy life, it seems, is not some rare discovery, but a combination of simple things many of us already know: eat well, keep moving, stay connected to others, and live with purpose. We do not need to find the secret; we need only to live it.

questions.prompt: What did scientists hope to discover by studying these regions?
options: The secret of a long, healthy life.

questions.prompt: How do the long-lived stay physically active?
options: Through hard exercise at a gym.
Through natural daily movement like walking and gardening.
They do not move at all.

questions.prompt: What non-physical factors matter, according to scientists?
options: Respect for elders, friendship, belonging, and a sense of purpose.

questions.prompt: What is the lesson of these communities?
options: That long life needs a rare discovery.
That the secret is a combination of simple things we already know.
That diet is the only thing that matters.
That a long life is impossible.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 长寿健康的秘密)、细节(B 走路/园艺等自然日常活动)、细节(B 受尊重、友谊、归属、目标感)、主旨(B 秘密在于我们早已知道的简单之事)。

sets.passage: In a struggling neighbourhood with few places to gather, a retired teacher named Mr. Davies started something small that grew into something remarkable: a community theatre. He had no money and no stage, only a belief that ordinary people had stories worth telling. At first, only a handful of people came to his meetings in a borrowed church hall. They were an unlikely group — shy teenagers, lonely pensioners, tired parents — with no acting experience among them. But Mr. Davies encouraged them to write and perform plays about their own lives and their own community. Slowly, the group grew. The effect on those who took part was profound. A shy boy who had barely spoken in class found his voice on stage. An elderly widow, lonely since her husband's death, found new friends and a reason to leave the house. People who had felt invisible discovered that they had something to say, and that others wanted to listen. The theatre also changed the neighbourhood itself. The plays, performed to growing audiences, brought people together and gave the community a new sense of pride. Neighbours who had never spoken became friends. What had begun as a hobby became a kind of glue, holding a fragile community together. Mr. Davies often said that he had not really created a theatre at all. 'I just gave people a stage,' he would say, 'and let them discover what they already had inside.' His story shows how one person, with little more than belief and energy, can help a whole community find its voice.

questions.prompt: What did Mr. Davies start?
What did the group perform?
options: Plays about their own lives and community.

questions.prompt: What effect did the theatre have on its members?
options: They found their voice, new friends, and a sense of being heard.
It cost them a lot of money.

questions.prompt: What does Mr. Davies's story show?
options: That one person, with belief and energy, can help a community find its voice.
That acting needs years of experience.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 社区剧团)、细节(B 演自己生活与社区的戏)、细节(B 找到了声音、朋友与被倾听感)、主旨(B 一个有信念与热情的人能帮整个社区发声)。
